intro: Senir (also Shenir) was the Amorite name for Mount Hermon (Deut. 3:9; 1 Chr. 5:23), though Deuteronomy notes that the Sidonians called the same mountain Sirion. The name may have applied originally to the entire Hermon range or to a specific peak within it. The forest slopes of Senir produced fir trees prized for shipbuilding, exported to Tyre (Ezek. 27:5). The Song of Solomon invites the beloved to "look from the top of Amana, from the top of Senir and Hermon" (Song 4:8), evoking the dramatic northern landscape of Syria. The region was part of the territory of Og king of Bashan conquered by Moses (Deut. 3:8).
